coredata.dc:title: Preparation of highly luminescent nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots and their application as a probe for detection of Staphylococcus aureus and E. coli
coredata.dc:creator: Safardoust-Hojaghan, Hossein
Salavati-Niasari, Masoud
Amiri, Omid
Hassanpour, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: The rapid detection of bacterial pathogens is very important for medical diagnostics, biosecurity, transportation and the prevention of global pandemics. Here graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are prepared by using citric acid and ethylenediamine and hydrothermal as starting materials and synthesis method. Product is characterized by X-ray diffraction analysis, FT-IR spectroscopy, Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. In the following, graphene quantum dots are used as photoluminescence sensor for detecting Staphylococcus aureus and E. coli. It is found that graphene quantum dots are sensitive at the range of 0 to 9×107 range for Staphylococcus aureus and E. coli.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanostructures
Graphene quantum dots
Biosensor
Staphylococcus aureus
Escherichia coli
Photoluminescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TGA and DTA analysis of GQDs.
a) PL analysis of prepared GQDs under different excitation wavelength b) Uv-Vis and PL spectrum of GQDs.
TEM image of a) isolated GQDs b) stacked GQDs.
Quenching of GQDs with increasing amount of Staph. Aureus. Inset: linear correlation between concentrations of Staph. Aureus and PL intensity.
Quenching of GQDs with increasing amount of E. coli. Inset: linear correlation between concentration of E. coli and PL intensity.
Bacteria extraction from herbal medicine manufactory wastewater.
Formation mechanism of Graphene quantum dots from citric acid and ethylenediamine.

head.ce:title: Preparation of highly luminescent nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots and their application as a probe for detection of Staphylococcus aureus and E. coli

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology, University of Kashan, Kashan, P.O. Box 87317-51167, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science, University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rapid detection of bacterial pathogens is very important for medical diagnostics, biosecurity, transportation and the prevention of global pandemics. Here graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are prepared by using citric acid and ethylenediamine and hydrothermal as starting materials and synthesis method. Product is characterized by X-ray diffraction analysis, FT-IR spectroscopy, Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. In the following, graphene quantum dots are used as photoluminescence sensor for detecting Staphylococcus aureus and E. coli. It is found that graphene quantum dots are sensitive at the range of 0 to 9×107 range for Staphylococcus aureus and E. coli.

ce:section.ce:para: Staphylococcus aureus is a gram-positive and facultative anaerobe bacterium that is the most important species of Staphylococcus [1]. This bacterium is frequently found in the respiratory tract, nose, and on the skin. These bacteria produce yellowish pigment called staphyloxanthin that has important role in diseases because it acts as anti-oxidant and help bacteria to survive against oxygen free radical [2]. Oxygen free radical are released by white blood cells of host body in order to kill bacteria. We found that this bacterium could survive at 90°C for 15min.

Staphylococcus cause a wide range of infections from simple skin infections such as rash and boil to life- threating diseases such as pneumonia, meningitis, osteomyelitis, endocarditis, toxic shock syndrome and septicemia. Staphylococcus aureus is one of the five most common causes of nosocomial infections. Every year, 500 thousand people in the United States of America are infected with Staphylococcus aureus.

Bacterial metabolic tests are standard protocols for bacterial identification in use by most hospitals. However, this process sometimes takes days for identification of the pathogenic bacteria that is unacceptable delay in emergency and critically ill situations such as sepsis. For this reason, several ultra-sensitive detection methods based on nucleic acid amplification, such as LCR (ligase chain reaction) [3], PCR (polymerase chain reaction) [4], and SDA (strand displacement amplification) [5], have been introduced. All of these technologies are able to detect low numbers of bacterial cells within several hours. These technologies require prior isolation of bacterial DNA, expensive instruments for nucleic acid amplification, and preparation of enzyme reaction mix. These high costs and complex procedures limit the widespread use of these technologies for clinical diagnosis. The rapid detection of bacterial pathogens is very important for medical diagnostics, biosecurity, transportation and the prevention of global pandemics [6,7]. This is a major public health concern because infection constitutes the major cause of death.

Escherichia coli (also known as E. coli) is a facultative anaerobic, Gram-negative, rod-shaped bacterium of the genus Escherichia that is commonly found in the lower intestine of warm-blooded organisms. Most E. coli strains are harmless, but some serotypes can cause serious food poisoning in their hosts. E. coli is expelled into the environment within fecal matter. The bacterium grows massively in fresh fecal matter under aerobic conditions for 3days.

Here we use graphene quantum dots for detecting Staphylococcus aureus and E. coli. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work that graphene quantum dots are used for detecting Staphylococcus aureus and E. coli. We use citric acid as precursor for prepare carbon dots.

In recent years, graphene quantum dots have been widely applied in the fields of catalyst [8,9], cell imaging [10,11] and so on, due to their special advantages such as easy preparation [12] and high quantum yield. The unique optical properties of graphene quantum dots make them very promising as fluorescent probes in fluorescence detection. A number of studies have reported that using graphene quantum dots as fluorescent probes could improve detection limit of metal ions [13–18]. Yang et al. proposed a novel sensitive method for detection of tetracyclines using graphene quantum dots as fluorescent probes [19].

For characterization of GQDs, X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded by a Philips-X'PertPro, X-ray diffractometer using Ni-filtered Cu Kα radiation at scan range of 10<2θ<80. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained on a Philips EM208S transmission electron microscope with an accelerating voltage of 100kV. UV-vis spectroscopy analysis. GC-2550TG (Teif Gostar Faraz Company, Iran) were used for all chemical analyses. The entire chemicals used in this investigation were of analytical grade: Citric acid (99.5%), ethylene diamine (99%) from Merck. Staphylococcus aureus and E. coli were extracted from herbal medicine manufactory wastewater. Gram-negative E. coli and Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus were cultured on LB agar plates, then they were isolated and characterized (Scheme 1 ). In typical synthesis, certain amount of citric acid and ethylenediamine were dissolved DI water, and stirred to form a clear solution. Then the solution was transferred into Teflon lined stainless autoclave. Different parameters such as time, temperature, and ratio of citric acid to ethylenediamine were studied. The optimum condition was 6h, 180°C, and 1:1 of citric acid: ethylenediamine, respectively. The final product was soluble in water. A fixed amount of graphene quantum dots was transferred to a main flask, and then predetermined volume of Staph. Aureus and E. coli was added to flask. Solution was transferred to a fluorescent cuvette and the fluorescent intensity of the solution was recorded.

Properties of graphene quantum dots and carbon dots such as optical properties strongly depend on preparation method and precursors. Here graphene quantum dots are prepared by using citric acid and ethylenediamine and hydrothermal as starting materials and synthesis method, respectively. Scheme 2 shows formation mechanism of GQDs from citric acid and ethylenediamine [20,21]. This is the first time that graphene quantum dots are prepared by this method and these starting materials. As-synthesized graphene quantum dots show high quantum yield, which make them a good candidate for fluorescent probes in fluorescence detection. Pure graphene quantum dots which prepared by using only citric acid as precursor show low quantum yield and stability. On the other word, they do not show fluorescence after two months. Therefore, we use ethylenediamine to dope N into graphene quantum dots. Doped graphene quantum dots show higher quantum yield and stability as they show fluorescence after 9months. Quantum Yield can be expressed by Eq. 1 [22]:
(1)
φ = φ
r
× A
r
/ I
r
× I / A × n
2
/ n
r
2
Where φ is the Quantum Yield; also φr is the Quantum Yield of reference that is Rhodamine B and equal to 0.31. The Ar and Ir represent absorption and integral area under the curve for reference, respectively. The A and I show absorption and integral area under the curve for sample, respectively. Quantum Yield for prepared Graphene quantum dots is 25%.

Fig. 1 shows the XRD pattern of as-synthesized graphene quantum dots. Presence of broad peak at 2θ=25 (0.42nm) is related to graphitic structure of GQDs with no impurity. Fig. 2 shows FT-IR spectrum of GQDs. Peaks at 1690 and 3480cm−1 attribute to carboxyl and hydroxyl group respectively. Peak at 1270cm−1 corroborate presence of amid group on surface of GQDs.

Fig. 3 shows thermogravimetric analysis of GQDs.as well as shown, 10% of initial weight of GQDs loss up to 150°C that related to thermal desorption of physically adsorbed water molecules on GQDs surface. Main weight loss is occurred at 150–600°C attributable to functional group such as –COOH groups at the edge and –OH groups on the basal plane [23].

Uv-Vis and PL spectrum of graphene quantum dots are shown in Fig. 4 . as well as shown in Fig. 4, the GQDs shows absorption bands at 400nm which is attributed to π-π* transition of graphene quantum dots. About PL mechanism of GQDs, two main reasons are proposed. First, surface/edge state in GQDs that explains when graphene is cutting along different directions, armchair and zigzag edges can be otained. Edge types is more important key in electronic properties of GQDs. In addition, oxygen and amine based groups on surface of GQDs affect optical band gap. For this reason, synthesis method and starting materials play important role on electronic properties of GQDs. Second is attributed to quantum confinement in GQDs [24]. As well as shown in Fig. 4, PL intensity centered in 565nm was obtained with the excitation wavelength of 330nm. Inset of Fig. 4 shows synthesized GQDs under day light and UV light (366nm).

Fig. 5 illustrate TEM of graphene quantum dots. TEM images of the resulting GQDs show that these GQDs are uniform in size ranging from 15 to 20nm in diameter (Fig. 5a). According to the Fig. 5b, GQDs stacking to form large particles. Qin Li and et al. report that this happens because at higher temperature most of tiny particles stack and form larger particles with inhomogeneous sizes [25].

This is the first time that graphene quantum dots were used for detecting Staphylococcus aureus. Under the optimized operational conditions, fluorescence signals of different concentrations of GQDs-labeled Staphylococcus aureus and E. coli were detected. This results show that amount of unknown concentration of these bacteria at this range, can be measured as well. GQDs are tiny size fragments of graphene, this quantum confinement converts GQDs to non-zero band gap semiconductor that have PL on excitation. These optical properties of GQDs intensively depend on defects, size of GQDs, functional groups on surface and oxygen groups. Introducing of bacteria into GQDs solution can be involved surface functional group and so quenching was occurred [20]. Fig. 6 and Fig. 7 show the quenching of GQDs PL with increasing amount of Staph aureus and E. coli bacteria respectively. Inset of Fig. 6 and Fig. 7 represents linear correlation of PL intensity and bacteria concentration. These correlations obtain from Stern-Volmer relationship as shown in Eq. (2):
(2)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K SV
C
Where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities recorded in absence and presence of bacteria, respectively, [C] is the concentration of bacteria and Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. A standard curve was obtained in the concentration range from 0 to 9×107 CFU·ml−1. For convenient interpretation, we use log[C] instead of [C]. Linear equation for Staph. aureus and E. coli is obtained y=0.950×−6.375 and y=0.658×−3.993.

In summary, graphene Quantum Dots with high quantum yield were prepared by using citric acid and ethylenediamine. These prepared Graphene Quantum Dots show long-term fluorescence stability. In this paper, a rapid and sensitive method for quantitative detection of Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli using graphene carbon dots as a novel fluorescent probe had been successfully established. The graphene quantum dots modified with ethylenediamine were prepared in a very simple way and its quantum yield was as high as ca. 25%. This proposed method was easy in operation and could finish detection within 2h with high sensitivity and specificity. There was a linear relation between the fluorescence intensity and concentrations of Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli in the range of 0 to 9×107 CFU·ml−1 respectively.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are grateful to the council of Iran National Science Foundation (INSF) and University of Kashan for supporting this work by Grant No (159271/89920).
